clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 6-month history of heavy menstrual bleeding that is affecting her quality of life, forcing her to take days off work each month. She has no intermenstrual or post-coital bleeding. Examination reveals a normal-sized uterus and a normal speculum examination. FBC shows Hb 10.8 g/dL. Transvaginal ultrasound reveals a normal uterus with a 5 mm regular endometrial stripe. She has no current contraceptive needs but is open to hormonal options. She has no red-flag symptoms. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (Mirena)
Oral tranexamic acid alone
Refer immediately for endometrial biopsy
Commence combined oral contraceptive pill
Refer for hysterectomy
explanation: According to NICE guideline NG88, the Levonorgestrel-IUS (Mirena) is the first-line treatment for heavy menstrual bleeding when there is no structural cause, no red flags (such as IMB or PMB), and a normal TVUS. It reduces menstrual blood loss by 90% or more and provides contraception. Endometrial biopsy is not indicated here as there are no red-flag symptoms and the TVUS is non-suspicious. Tranexamic acid and NSAIDs are alternatives, not first-line. Hysterectomy is a last resort after medical management has failed.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. LNG-IUS (Mirena) is the NICE-recommended first-line treatment for HMB with no structural cause and no red flags.
Tranexamic acid is a valid alternative but is not NICE first-line when the patient is open to hormonal options; LNG-IUS is preferred.
Endometrial biopsy is NOT indicated in isolated HMB with a normal TVUS and no red flags such as IMB, PMB, or suspicious endometrial thickness.
COCP is an alternative option but not the NICE first-line treatment for HMB in this scenario.
Hysterectomy is only considered after medical management has failed; it would be inappropriate at this stage.
clinicalPearl: For HMB with normal TVUS and no red flags, LNG-IUS (Mirena) is the NICE first-line treatment — not endometrial biopsy.
keyClue: Normal uterus, 5 mm regular endometrium, no IMB, no PMB — no red flags, structural or malignant pathology excluded.
commonExamTrap: Ordering an endometrial biopsy for every woman with HMB — sampling is only indicated for red-flag features (PMB, persistent IMB, suspicious TVUS findings, or high-risk profile).
missedConcept: AUB is a symptom, not a diagnosis; management is guided by risk stratification, not automatic investigation of all patients.

clinicalStem: A 64-year-old woman presents to the gynaecology clinic with a single episode of vaginal bleeding. She reached menopause 10 years ago and has not bled since. She takes no hormone replacement therapy. On examination she appears well, vital signs are stable, and bimanual examination is unremarkable. Transvaginal ultrasound reveals an endometrial thickness of 9 mm with an irregular lining. Which single next investigation is most appropriate?
options: Hysteroscopy with targeted biopsy
Blind Pipelle endometrial biopsy alone
Reassure and discharge — single episode is likely atrophic
Pelvic MRI as first-line imaging
Serum CA-125 and repeat TVUS in 3 months
explanation: Any post-menopausal bleeding mandates endometrial sampling. The TVUS shows an endometrial thickness of 9 mm (the red-flag threshold is >4–5 mm in PMB) with an irregular lining. NICE guidance recommends proceeding directly to hysteroscopy with targeted biopsy rather than blind Pipelle in this scenario, because the Pipelle can miss focal lesions and has a higher inadequate-sample rate in post-menopausal women. Hysteroscopy is the gold standard, allowing direct visualisation and targeted sampling.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Hysteroscopy with targeted biopsy is the gold standard when PMB is associated with a thickened or irregular endometrium on TVUS.
Blind Pipelle biopsy has a high rate of inadequate samples in post-menopausal women and can miss focal lesions; hysteroscopy is preferred when the endometrium is thickened or irregular.
PMB must never be dismissed as atrophic without investigation; it carries a significant risk of endometrial carcinoma and investigation is mandatory.
MRI is used when ultrasound is inconclusive, not as first-line imaging; TVUS has already confirmed a suspicious finding.
CA-125 is not part of the standard initial work-up for PMB; immediate endometrial sampling is required.
clinicalPearl: Any post-menopausal bleeding with endometrial thickness >4 mm requires endometrial tissue sampling; hysteroscopy with targeted biopsy is the gold standard.
keyClue: Single episode of PMB + endometrial thickness 9 mm + irregular lining on TVUS.
commonExamTrap: Choosing blind Pipelle over hysteroscopy when the endometrium is thickened or irregular — Pipelle misses focal lesions.
missedConcept: PMB is a red-flag symptom that mandates investigation regardless of how trivial the bleeding appears; a single episode is never 'just atrophic' without tissue diagnosis.
redFlagReason: Post-menopausal bleeding with thickened endometrium carries a significant risk of endometrial carcinoma; delayed investigation is dangerous.

subtopic: Biopsy result interpretation and next steps
clinicalStem: A 55-year-old obese woman (BMI 38) presents with a 4-month history of irregular vaginal bleeding. She is 2 years post-menopause. She has PCOS and is nulliparous. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a thickened endometrium at 11 mm. She proceeds to hysteroscopy and targeted biopsy. Histology returns: 'atypical endometrial hyperplasia'. She states clearly that she has completed her family. What is the most appropriate next management step?
options: Refer to gynaecology for total hysterectomy
Insert Levonorgestrel-IUS and repeat biopsy in 6 months
Start high-dose oral progestogens and follow-up annually
Repeat hysteroscopy with wider excision of abnormal tissue
Prescribe combined oral contraceptive pill and reassess in 3 months
explanation: Atypical endometrial hyperplasia (AEH) carries a high risk of progression to endometrial carcinoma (up to 29% may co-exist with carcinoma at hysterectomy). For a woman who has completed her family, the definitive and recommended management is total hysterectomy. High-dose progestogens and LNG-IUS are fertility-sparing alternatives reserved only for women who strongly desire future pregnancy or are unfit for surgery. In this post-menopausal nulliparous woman who has completed her family, hysterectomy is the correct answer.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Total hysterectomy is the definitive treatment for AEH in a woman who has completed her family, due to the high risk of progression to endometrial cancer.
LNG-IUS with 6-monthly biopsy is appropriate for endometrial hyperplasia WITHOUT atypia (low-risk pre-cancerous stage), not atypical hyperplasia in a woman who does not desire fertility.
High-dose progestogens are the fertility-sparing option for AEH but are only appropriate when the woman desires future pregnancy; this patient has completed her family.
Wider excision at hysteroscopy does not treat the diffuse endometrial disease and is not a recognised definitive management for AEH.
COCP has no established role in the management of atypical endometrial hyperplasia.
clinicalPearl: Atypical endometrial hyperplasia requires total hysterectomy if the woman has completed her family; fertility-sparing progestogen therapy is reserved for those who wish to conceive.
keyClue: Histology confirms ATYPICAL hyperplasia in a post-menopausal woman who has completed her family.
commonExamTrap: Treating atypical hyperplasia the same as hyperplasia without atypia (with LNG-IUS and surveillance) — these are fundamentally different risk categories.
missedConcept: The key distinction is between hyperplasia without atypia (managed conservatively with LNG-IUS) and atypical hyperplasia (high malignant potential, requires hysterectomy unless fertility is desired).

clinicalStem: A 26-year-old woman presents with a 12-month history of irregular, heavy menstrual cycles ranging from 21 to 45 days apart. She has no IMB or PCB. She has a known diagnosis of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Her BMI is 31. Examination is unremarkable. FBC shows Hb 11.2 g/dL. Transvaginal ultrasound shows polycystic-appearing ovaries and a normal uterus with a 7 mm endometrium. Pregnancy test is negative. According to the FIGO PALM-COEIN classification, which category best describes the most likely cause of her AUB?
options: AUB-O (Ovulatory dysfunction)
AUB-P (Polyp)
AUB-A (Adenomyosis)
AUB-C (Coagulopathy)
AUB-L (Leiomyoma)
explanation: In a young woman with PCOS, the underlying mechanism of AUB is anovulation leading to irregular, unopposed oestrogen stimulation of the endometrium without a progesterone withdrawal bleed. This falls under the 'O' category of COEIN — Ovulatory Dysfunction (AUB-O). There are no structural findings (polyp, fibroid, adenomyosis) on ultrasound, and there is no history suggesting a coagulopathy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. PCOS causes anovulation, leading to AUB-O — irregular bleeding due to disordered ovulation.
AUB-P (polyp) would require ultrasound evidence of an endometrial polyp; the ultrasound here shows a normal uterus without a polypoid lesion.
AUB-A (adenomyosis) typically presents with dysmenorrhoea and a bulky, tender uterus in an older multiparous woman; the ultrasound here is normal.
AUB-C (coagulopathy) should only be investigated if HMB began at menarche or there is a personal/family history of bleeding disorders; this is not suggested here.
AUB-L (leiomyoma) requires ultrasound confirmation of fibroids; the uterus is described as normal on TVUS.
clinicalPearl: PCOS causes AUB through anovulation — the 'O' (Ovulatory Dysfunction) category of the FIGO PALM-COEIN classification.
keyClue: Irregular cycles, PCOS diagnosis, no structural lesion on TVUS.
commonExamTrap: Ordering routine hormonal or thyroid tests for every AUB patient — NICE advises against this unless symptoms specifically suggest thyroid disease or PCOS features are present.
missedConcept: AUB is a symptom; the PALM-COEIN classification guides the clinician to identify the underlying structural or non-structural cause.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of intermenstrual bleeding. She is obese (BMI 42) with a history of PCOS. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an endometrial thickness of 14 mm. She proceeds to hysteroscopy and biopsy. Histology reports: 'endometrial hyperplasia WITHOUT atypia'. She has completed her family. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Insert Levonorgestrel-IUS and repeat endometrial biopsy in 6 months
Refer immediately for total hysterectomy
Start combined oral contraceptive pill and reassess in 3 months
Reassure and discharge — this is a benign finding
Refer to gynaecology-oncology for staging
explanation: Endometrial hyperplasia without atypia is a low-risk pre-cancerous condition. Per RCOG guidelines, the treatment of choice is the Levonorgestrel-IUS (Mirena), which achieves regression of hyperplasia in the majority of cases. Surveillance with repeat biopsy at 6 months is mandatory to confirm regression. Hysterectomy is reserved for atypical hyperplasia or failed conservative treatment. Reassurance and discharge are inappropriate as this condition requires active management and surveillance.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. LNG-IUS plus 6-monthly biopsy surveillance is the standard management for endometrial hyperplasia without atypia.
Hysterectomy is appropriate for ATYPICAL hyperplasia, not for simple hyperplasia without atypia, especially as first-line management.
COCP is not the recognised treatment for endometrial hyperplasia without atypia; LNG-IUS is the preferred hormonal intervention.
Endometrial hyperplasia without atypia is a pre-cancerous state that requires active treatment and surveillance — discharge is inappropriate.
Gynaecology-oncology referral is required for confirmed malignancy, not for hyperplasia without atypia.
clinicalPearl: Endometrial hyperplasia without atypia is treated with LNG-IUS and monitored with repeat biopsy at 6 months — NOT hysterectomy.
keyClue: Histology confirms hyperplasia WITHOUT atypia — low-risk pre-cancerous stage.
commonExamTrap: Conflating hyperplasia without atypia with atypical hyperplasia and recommending hysterectomy for both.
missedConcept: Risk stratification of endometrial hyperplasia is essential: without atypia (LNG-IUS + surveillance) versus with atypia (hysterectomy unless fertility desired).

topic: Placental abruption — emergency recognition and management
subtopic: Pathological obstetric cause of abdominal pain
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 34 weeks gestation is brought to the emergency department by ambulance. She reports sudden onset of severe, constant abdominal pain that began 30 minutes ago. She also notes dark vaginal bleeding. On examination she is pale, diaphoretic, and in visible distress. BP is 90/60 mmHg, HR 128 bpm. Abdominal examination reveals a tender, rigid uterus that feels like a 'wooden board'. CTG shows late decelerations. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Resuscitate with IV fluids and blood products, commence continuous fetal monitoring, and arrange urgent delivery
Commence tocolysis with nifedipine to prevent preterm labour
Perform transvaginal ultrasound to confirm placental location before any intervention
Administer IM betamethasone for fetal lung maturity and observe for 24 hours
Perform digital cervical examination to assess for cord prolapse
explanation: This presentation — painful vaginal bleeding with a hard, woody uterus, haemodynamic instability, and fetal distress on CTG — is the classic picture of severe placental abruption. This is an obstetric emergency. The priority is simultaneous maternal resuscitation (IV access, fluid resuscitation, crossmatch, correct coagulopathy) and urgent delivery, which may need to be by emergency caesarean section given the fetal distress. Tocolysis is absolutely contraindicated in placental abruption. Ultrasound does not reliably exclude abruption and must not delay resuscitation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Severe placental abruption requires immediate maternal stabilisation and urgent delivery — this is a life-threatening emergency for both mother and fetus.
Tocolysis is absolutely contraindicated in placental abruption; suppressing contractions would increase concealed haemorrhage and worsen maternal and fetal outcomes.
Ultrasound has poor sensitivity for abruption and must not delay resuscitation; the clinical diagnosis is already compelling and haemodynamic instability takes priority.
Steroids are appropriate for threatened preterm labour with fetal well-being, not in an emergency where there is fetal distress and maternal haemodynamic compromise requiring immediate delivery.
Digital examination with antepartum haemorrhage should never be performed without excluding placenta praevia first; moreover, the priority here is resuscitation and delivery, not cervical assessment.
clinicalPearl: The triad of painful APH, woody-hard uterus, and fetal distress = severe placental abruption: resuscitate and deliver urgently. Tocolysis is absolutely contraindicated.
keyClue: Painful APH + rigid woody uterus + haemodynamic instability + fetal distress on CTG at 34 weeks.
commonExamTrap: Performing ultrasound or giving tocolytics before stabilising a haemodynamically unstable patient with suspected abruption.
missedConcept: Placental abruption is a clinical diagnosis; ultrasound is unreliable and must not delay emergency management.
redFlagReason: Severe placental abruption causes life-threatening maternal haemorrhage, DIC, and fetal death if not treated within minutes — immediate resuscitation and delivery are required.

topic: HELLP syndrome — recognition and emergency priority
subtopic: Pathological obstetric cause of abdominal pain
clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman at 32 weeks gestation is admitted with a 2-day history of severe epigastric pain, nausea, and vomiting. Her BP is 158/104 mmHg. She has 2+ proteinuria on urine dipstick. Blood results show: Hb 9.1 g/dL with evidence of haemolysis on blood film, AST 310 U/L (elevated), platelets 72 x10^9/L (low). What is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the immediate management priority?
options: HELLP syndrome — immediate delivery after maternal stabilisation with magnesium sulphate and antihypertensives
Acute fatty liver of pregnancy — commence IV glucose and supportive care
Acute appendicitis — arrange urgent surgical consultation and appendectomy
Acute pancreatitis — commence IV fluids, nil by mouth, and analgesia
Viral hepatitis — send hepatitis serology and arrange hepatology review
explanation: HELLP syndrome (Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is a severe variant of pre-eclampsia. The diagnostic triad is confirmed here: haemolysis on film, elevated AST, and thrombocytopaenia, in the context of hypertension and proteinuria. HELLP is an obstetric emergency. The definitive treatment is delivery. Before delivery, the mother must be stabilised with IV magnesium sulphate (to prevent eclampsia) and antihypertensive therapy (labetalol or hydralazine IV). Delayed delivery risks maternal complications including hepatic rupture, renal failure, and DIC.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. HELLP syndrome requires urgent delivery after maternal stabilisation with magnesium sulphate and antihypertensives.
Acute fatty liver of pregnancy presents with hypoglycaemia, elevated ammonia, and coagulopathy; haemolysis and isolated thrombocytopenia with hypertension/proteinuria fits HELLP more precisely.
Appendicitis does not explain hypertension, proteinuria, haemolytic anaemia, elevated LFTs, and thrombocytopenia together.
Pancreatitis would show markedly elevated amylase/lipase; the combination of haemolysis, elevated AST, and low platelets with pre-eclampsia is diagnostic of HELLP.
Viral hepatitis does not cause thrombocytopenia with haemolysis and would not explain the hypertension and proteinuria.
clinicalPearl: HELLP = Haemolysis + Elevated Liver enzymes + Low Platelets, occurring in the context of pre-eclampsia; definitive treatment is urgent delivery.
keyClue: Epigastric pain + hypertension + proteinuria + haemolysis + elevated AST + thrombocytopenia at 32 weeks.
commonExamTrap: Misattributing the epigastric pain to gastritis, pancreatitis, or cholecystitis without checking for the full blood picture including platelets and LFTs.
missedConcept: Any pregnant woman with epigastric pain and hypertension requires a full blood count and liver function tests to exclude HELLP syndrome.
redFlagReason: HELLP syndrome can cause hepatic rupture, DIC, renal failure, and maternal death; urgent delivery is the only definitive treatment.

subtopic: Non-obstetric surgical cause of abdominal pain
clinicalStem: A 25-year-old woman at 24 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of right-sided abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and a low-grade fever of 37.9°C. The pain initially started centrally and has moved to the right side of the abdomen. On examination, she has tenderness in the right upper quadrant rather than McBurney's point. WBC is 16.5 x10^9/L. Urinalysis is normal. Which of the following best explains the atypical presentation?
options: The gravid uterus displaces the appendix superiorly and to the right, shifting the site of maximal tenderness away from McBurney's point
The appendix perforates earlier in pregnant women, causing diffuse peritonitis before localisation
Pregnancy-induced immunosuppression blunts the inflammatory response, preventing pain
The sigmoid colon compresses the appendix in pregnancy, reducing the severity of pain
The appendix is physiologically removed from the peritoneum in pregnancy and therefore rarely causes tenderness
explanation: As pregnancy progresses, the growing uterus displaces the appendix superiorly and to the right, so the point of maximal tenderness in appendicitis moves from McBurney's point (RLQ) to the right upper quadrant or right flank. This displacement makes diagnosis difficult and means that 1 in 5 cases of appendicitis in pregnant women are not diagnosed until the appendix has ruptured, causing peritonitis. Any right-sided abdominal pain with fever, nausea, vomiting, and leucocytosis in a pregnant patient should prompt urgent surgical evaluation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. The gravid uterus displaces the appendix cranially and laterally, causing the site of tenderness to shift superiorly from McBurney's point as pregnancy advances.
While perforation does occur and is more dangerous in pregnancy, this does not explain the atypical location of tenderness; that is explained by physical displacement.
Pregnancy does not cause immunosuppression that blunts pain perception to this degree; the atypical location is anatomical, not immunological.
The sigmoid colon is on the left side and does not compress the appendix; this option is anatomically inaccurate.
The appendix is displaced but remains intraperitoneal and is capable of causing localised peritonism; it is not removed from the peritoneum.
clinicalPearl: In pregnancy, the appendix migrates superiorly and to the right with the growing uterus, so the pain of appendicitis may localise to the RUQ rather than McBurney's point — a classic exam trap.
keyClue: Right upper quadrant tenderness (not RLQ) in a pregnant woman with fever, vomiting, and leucocytosis.
commonExamTrap: Dismissing appendicitis because the pain is not at McBurney's point in a pregnant patient.
missedConcept: Appendicitis in pregnancy: 1 in 1500 pregnancies, 1 in 5 cases missed until perforation; the diagnosis is complicated by upward displacement of the appendix.

clinicalStem: A 31-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation presents to the antenatal clinic with moderate lower back pain and mild pelvic girdle pain. She asks her midwife for analgesia to help manage her discomfort at home. She is otherwise well, with no red-flag symptoms, and the pain is musculoskeletal in nature. Which of the following is the most appropriate analgesic for use in this situation?
options: Paracetamol
Ibuprofen
Diclofenac
Aspirin 600 mg
Codeine 30 mg regularly
explanation: Paracetamol is the safest and most appropriate first-choice analgesic throughout pregnancy for musculoskeletal pain. NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac) are contraindicated after 20 weeks gestation due to the risk of premature closure of the ductus arteriosus, oligohydramnios, and neonatal pulmonary hypertension. High-dose aspirin is also an NSAID with similar risks. Opioids such as codeine may be used with caution for acute severe pain but are not appropriate for regular use in non-emergency musculoskeletal pain due to risks of neonatal withdrawal.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Paracetamol is the first-line analgesic in pregnancy and is considered safe at all stages.
Ibuprofen is an NSAID and is contraindicated after 20 weeks gestation due to the risk of premature closure of the ductus arteriosus and oligohydramnios.
Diclofenac is an NSAID; its use after 20 weeks gestation is contraindicated for the same reasons as ibuprofen.
High-dose aspirin (analgesic doses) carries NSAID-related risks in late pregnancy; low-dose aspirin (75–150 mg) is used prophylactically for pre-eclampsia but analgesic doses are contraindicated.
Regular opioid use in pregnancy carries risks of neonatal withdrawal syndrome and respiratory depression; codeine should not be used for routine musculoskeletal pain when paracetamol is sufficient.
clinicalPearl: NSAIDs are contraindicated after 20 weeks gestation — paracetamol is the safe first-choice analgesic throughout pregnancy.
keyClue: 28 weeks gestation — musculoskeletal pain requiring safe home analgesia.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing ibuprofen for pain relief in a third-trimester pregnant woman without considering the contraindication after 20 weeks.
missedConcept: NSAID use after 20 weeks of pregnancy can cause oligohydramnios and premature closure of the ductus arteriosus — both are serious fetal complications.

subtopic: Pathological obstetric cause of abdominal pain
clinicalStem: A 27-year-old woman at 30 weeks gestation presents to the labour ward with regular uterine contractions every 4 minutes for the past 2 hours, accompanied by lower back pain radiating to the suprapubic region. Speculum examination confirms cervical dilatation of 2 cm with intact membranes. CTG is reassuring. Fetal presentation is cephalic on ultrasound. She has no fever, and fetal movements are normal. What is the most appropriate initial management bundle?
options: Administer tocolytics, intramuscular corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity, and IV magnesium sulphate for neonatal neuroprotection
Proceed immediately to emergency caesarean section
Commence IV oxytocin to augment labour and facilitate early delivery
Administer oral antibiotics for group B Streptococcus prophylaxis only and observe
Perform amniotomy to accelerate labour and reduce maternal distress
explanation: At 30 weeks gestation, preterm labour is a significant cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality. The management bundle is: (1) tocolytics (e.g., nifedipine or atosiban) to delay delivery and allow time for steroids to work; (2) intramuscular corticosteroids (betamethasone) to accelerate fetal lung maturity and reduce the risk of respiratory distress syndrome; and (3) intravenous magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection of the preterm neonate (reduces risk of cerebral palsy). Emergency caesarean and augmentation are inappropriate unless there is a specific indication.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. The three-part bundle of tocolytics + corticosteroids + magnesium sulphate is the standard management of threatened preterm labour between 24–34 weeks.
Emergency caesarean section is not indicated in uncomplicated preterm labour with intact membranes and a reassuring CTG; tocolysis and fetal lung maturity measures come first.
Oxytocin augmentation would accelerate delivery of a very preterm neonate and is contraindicated in this situation.
GBS prophylaxis alone is insufficient; the priority is to delay delivery and protect the fetus with steroids and neuroprotection.
Amniotomy would accelerate delivery of a 30-week fetus, worsening prematurity outcomes; this is contraindicated in this scenario.
clinicalPearl: Preterm labour <34 weeks: tocolysis to buy time for steroids (fetal lung maturity) + magnesium sulphate (neuroprotection) — this is the classic management triad.
keyClue: 30 weeks gestation + regular contractions + cervical dilatation + reassuring CTG — threatened preterm labour.
commonExamTrap: Forgetting magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection, or confusing tocolysis with augmentation.
missedConcept: Magnesium sulphate given before 34 weeks reduces the risk of cerebral palsy in preterm neonates — it is neuroprotective, not a tocolytic.

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old woman at 33 weeks gestation presents with a 12-hour history of lower abdominal pain, fever of 38.7°C, and uterine tenderness on palpation. She reports a watery vaginal discharge for the past 2 days. Her WBC is 19.2 x10^9/L. CTG shows fetal tachycardia at 185 bpm. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Commence IV broad-spectrum antibiotics and arrange urgent delivery
Administer IM corticosteroids and tocolytics and observe for 24 hours
Perform amniocentesis to confirm diagnosis before starting treatment
Reassure the patient that uterine tenderness is normal at 33 weeks
Start oral amoxicillin and review in clinic in 48 hours
explanation: This presentation is consistent with chorioamnionitis (intra-amniotic infection): fever, uterine tenderness, purulent/watery vaginal discharge (suggestive of ruptured membranes), leucocytosis, and fetal tachycardia. Chorioamnionitis is a serious infection that threatens both maternal and fetal life. The management priorities are: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., ampicillin and gentamicin) and delivery, regardless of gestational age, because ongoing infection worsens neonatal and maternal outcomes. Tocolysis is contraindicated in the presence of infection. Amniocentesis is not needed when the clinical diagnosis is clear.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Chorioamnionitis requires immediate IV antibiotics and delivery — ongoing infection is the danger and must be treated urgently.
Tocolytics are absolutely contraindicated in chorioamnionitis; suppressing contractions prolongs fetal exposure to infection and worsens outcomes.
Amniocentesis is rarely needed when the clinical diagnosis of chorioamnionitis is already established; it would delay treatment unnecessarily.
Uterine tenderness with fever, leucocytosis, and fetal tachycardia is never normal at any gestation — this is a medical emergency.
Oral antibiotics are insufficient for chorioamnionitis, which requires IV therapy; outpatient review in 48 hours is dangerous and inappropriate.
clinicalPearl: Chorioamnionitis = fever + uterine tenderness + fetal tachycardia + leucocytosis: start IV antibiotics and deliver — tocolysis is contraindicated.
keyClue: Fever + uterine tenderness + watery vaginal discharge (ruptured membranes) + fetal tachycardia + markedly elevated WBC at 33 weeks.
commonExamTrap: Giving tocolytics in the setting of suspected infection to delay delivery — this is dangerous and contraindicated.
missedConcept: Chorioamnionitis is an indication for delivery, not for pregnancy prolongation; the infected uterine environment is harmful to both mother and fetus.
redFlagReason: Untreated chorioamnionitis causes maternal sepsis, fetal sepsis, stillbirth, and neonatal death; immediate IV antibiotics and delivery are life-saving.

subtopic: Scarred uterus and sudden abdominal pain
clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman, G3P2, at 38 weeks gestation with two previous caesarean sections presents with sudden onset of severe generalised abdominal pain that started 20 minutes ago during an active labour contraction. The pain does not resolve between contractions. She is pale and distressed. BP is 80/50 mmHg, HR 140 bpm. CTG shows prolonged fetal bradycardia at 80 bpm. On palpation the uterus is no longer clearly palpable and the fetal parts are felt superficially under the abdominal wall. What is the single most appropriate immediate action?
options: Call for immediate help, activate the obstetric emergency team, and prepare for emergency laparotomy
Increase IV oxytocin to strengthen uterine contractions and facilitate vaginal delivery
Insert a urinary catheter, perform vaginal examination, and assess for cord prolapse
Administer IV magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection and transfer to ICU
Perform immediate transvaginal ultrasound to confirm placental location
explanation: This is a classic presentation of uterine rupture: previous scarred uterus (two caesarean sections), sudden severe abdominal pain not relieved between contractions, haemodynamic shock, fetal distress (prolonged bradycardia), and loss of uterine contour with fetal parts palpable superficially. Uterine rupture is an immediately life-threatening obstetric emergency requiring laparotomy within minutes to deliver the fetus and control haemorrhage. Any delay significantly increases maternal and fetal mortality. Oxytocin augmentation is absolutely contraindicated.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Uterine rupture requires immediate activation of the emergency team and emergency laparotomy — every minute counts for maternal and fetal survival.
IV oxytocin is absolutely contraindicated in uterine rupture; augmenting contractions would extend the tear and worsen haemorrhage catastrophically.
Vaginal examination and catheterisation should not delay emergency laparotomy; the diagnosis is clinical and the priority is the operating theatre.
Magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection is not the priority; haemorrhagic shock and fetal bradycardia require surgical control, not neuroprotection.
Transvaginal ultrasound would further delay surgery and is inappropriate when the clinical diagnosis of rupture is clear and the patient is in haemorrhagic shock.
clinicalPearl: Uterine rupture: scarred uterus + sudden tearing pain + haemodynamic collapse + loss of uterine contour = immediate emergency laparotomy, no delay.
keyClue: Two previous caesarean sections + sudden pain not relieved between contractions + haemodynamic shock + fetal bradycardia + fetal parts palpable superficially.
commonExamTrap: Performing further investigations (ultrasound, vaginal examination) before calling for emergency surgery when the clinical diagnosis of uterine rupture is already clear.
missedConcept: Uterine rupture most commonly occurs in a scarred uterus (previous caesarean section); the loss of uterine contour with superficial palpation of fetal parts is pathognomonic.
redFlagReason: Uterine rupture causes catastrophic haemorrhage and rapid fetal and maternal death if not treated with emergency laparotomy within minutes.
